text: Porous metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have been exploited for a wide variety of electronic device applications only occasionally so far, primarily because very few MOFs display electrical conductivity. We show for the first time the utilisation of a new class of electrically-conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs, employed as chemiresistive sensors for the identification of volatile organic compounds (VOCs). Starting with a series of highly uniform structural 2D MOFs we demonstrated the use of a sensor array of structurally analogous materials where cross-reactivity resulted in clear separation of different types of VOCs, exploiting multiple sensing mechanisms with highly orthogonal responses. Collectively, these observations suggest uniqueness of the MOFs properties as chemiresistor materials and suggest potential in sensor applications over more traditional resistors.
